perturbation.original_text: Each payment for a particular calendar month shall be due no later than the (20th) day of the subsequent calendar month.
perturbation.changed_text: Each payment for a particular calendar month shall be due no later than the (30th) day of the subsequent calendar month.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline to the 30th of the month may contradict New York's prompt payment laws if the customer is a government agency or if another law mandates faster payment.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Gen. Mun. Law § 30-a requires prompt payment to contractors by government entities. While the law allows some flexibility, a blanket 30-day payment term might not be compliant depending on the specific circumstances and statutory requirements for payment after invoice receipt or approval. The modification could violate the intent of prompt payment if it delays payment beyond what the law allows.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE V LICENSEE FEE, TAXES, AND TERM III. Time of Payment (a)
perturbation.justification: The modification from the 20th to the 30th of the month for payment due date creates a potential contradiction with prompt payment laws, specifically NY Gen. Mun. Law § 30-a, which requires prompt payment to contractors by government entities. The Law Explanation states that a blanket 30-day payment term might not be compliant depending on the specific circumstances and statutory requirements for payment after invoice receipt or approval, potentially violating the intent of prompt payment. The scraped snippet 1 shows a legislative history related to NY General Municipal Law, indicating this law is subject to change and interpretation, meaning a definitive payment deadline is important. Because of the possibility of a violation and the intent of prompt payment laws being undermined, the contradiction is present.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term of this Agreement and during the three year period after the expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Licensee will not solicit any person employed by Licensor and/or its Affiliates to leave his or her employment with Licensor.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term of this Agreement and during the five year period after the expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Licensee will not solicit any person employed by Licensor and/or its Affiliates to leave his or her employment with Licensor.
perturbation.explanation: The modified 5-year non-solicitation period may be unreasonably long and therefore unenforceable under California law, as it can unduly restrict employee mobility and competition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: CA Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: CA Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are exceptions, a 5-year non-solicitation agreement might be seen as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it is overly broad or burdensome. This is especially true because the company HQ is in New York, so an agreement like this might not make sense under the context of the original text.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change from a three-year to a five-year non-solicitation period creates a potential legal contradiction under California law. The original text's modification extends the restricted period, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The five-year period, compared to the three-year period, increases the likelihood that the agreement will be seen as an unreasonable restraint on an employee's ability to work. Therefore, the change makes it more likely that a court would find the agreement unenforceable under California law.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall prepare and submit summary quarterly reports to Licensor no later than 20 days following the last business day of each calendar quarter, which reports must specify a complete record of usage of the Licensed Technology by the Licensee and shall accompany the payments made to Licensor.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall prepare and submit summary quarterly reports to Licensor no later than 45 days following the last business day of each calendar quarter, which reports must specify a complete record of usage of the Licensed Technology by the Licensee and shall accompany the payments made to Licensor.
perturbation.explanation: While seemingly innocuous, delaying the report submission to 45 days may violate SEC regulations related to timely financial disclosures if SFG Financial Corp is a publicly traded company and this data is material to its financial reporting.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 outlines the requirements for quarterly reports (Form 10-Q) that publicly traded companies must file with the SEC. While this software license agreement isn't directly a financial report, if the data within these quarterly reports from the Licensee is critical for SFG Financial Corp's accurate and timely financial reporting, delaying the submission by increasing the window from 20 to 45 days after the quarter ends could indirectly impede its compliance with SEC regulations on timely financial disclosures.  The SEC mandates specific deadlines for filing quarterly reports, and delaying the receipt of key data needed for those reports could cause the filing to be late.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE V LICENSEE FEE, TAXES, AND TERM IV. Record Keeping and Reports (a)

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 - Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter). | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 - Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter). CFR prev | next § 240.13a-13 Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter). (a) Except as provided in paragraphs (b) and (c) of this section , every issuer that has securities registered pursuant to section 12 of the Act and is required to file annual reports pursuant to section 13 of the Act, and has filed or intends to file such reports on Form 10-K ( § 249.310 of this chapter), shall file a quarterly report on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter) within the period specified in General Instruction A.1. to that form for each of the first three quarters of each fiscal year of the issuer , commencing with the first fiscal quarter following the most recent fiscal year for which full financial statements were included in the registration statement , or, if the registration statement included financial statements for an interim period subsequent to the most recent fiscal year end meeting the requirements of Article 10 of Regulation S-X and Rule 8-03 of Regulation S-X for smaller reporting companies, for the first fiscal quarter subsequent to the quarter reported upon in the registration statement . The first quarterly report of the issuer shall be filed either within 45 days after the effective date of the registration statement or on or before the date on which such report would have been required to be filed if the issuer has been required to file reports on Form 10-Q as of its last fiscal quarter, whichever is later. (b) The provisions of this rule shall not apply to the following issuers: (1) Investment companies required to file reports pursuant to § 270.30a-1 ; (2) Foreign private issuers required to file reports pursuant to § 240.13a-16 ; and (3) Asset-backed issu
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the deadline for submitting quarterly reports from 20 to 45 days. The law explanation and perturbation explanation suggest this delay could hinder SFG Financial Corp's compliance with SEC regulations, particularly 17 CFR § 240.13a-13, regarding timely filing of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.13a-13, states that issuers 'shall file a quarterly report on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter) within the period specified in General Instruction A.1. to that form'. By potentially delaying the receipt of critical data, the contract change introduces an inconsistency that may violate federal law, fitting the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.
